headline: American Independence declared
context: The Continental Congress signs the Declaration of Independence. Thirteen colonies break from British rule. A new nation founded on the principle that all men are created equal — a promise it would spend centuries trying to keep.

headline: 13th Amendment abolishes slavery
context: August 18 1865. The American Civil War ends. Four million enslaved people are freed. The bloodiest conflict in American history closes — but the wound of racial inequality remains open for generations.

headline: Edison invents the light bulb
context: Thomas Edison develops a practical incandescent light bulb on October 21. One man's persistence against thousands of failures transforms how humanity lives after dark. The age of electricity begins.

headline: Statue of Liberty dedicated
context: October 28 1886. France gifts America the Statue of Liberty. A beacon of hope for millions of immigrants arriving by ship. Lady Liberty holds her torch above New York Harbor — a promise of freedom that not all who arrived would receive equally.

headline: Wright Brothers first flight
context: December 17 1903 at Kitty Hawk North Carolina. Twelve seconds. 120 feet. Orville Wright becomes the first human to achieve powered flight. Sixty six years later humanity would walk on the moon.

headline: John F Kennedy born
context: May 29 1917. The 35th President of the United States is born in Brookline Massachusetts. He would inspire a generation with Ask not what your country can do for you and be assassinated before seeing his vision fulfilled.

headline: Treaty of Versailles signed
context: January 19 1919. The peace treaty ending World War One is signed. Fifty million dead. The punishing terms imposed on Germany would plant the seeds for a second and even more devastating world war twenty years later.

headline: Alexander Fleming discovers penicillin
context: September 1928. A contaminated petri dish accidentally reveals that mould kills bacteria. Fleming discovers penicillin. Hundreds of millions of lives would be saved. One of the greatest accidents in scientific history.

headline: Wall Street Crash begins Great Depression
context: October 29 Black Tuesday. The stock market collapses. Millions lose everything overnight. Unemployment reaches 25 percent. Breadlines stretch across American cities. The Great Depression begins.

headline: D-Day Normandy landings
context: June 6 1944. 156000 Allied troops storm the beaches of Normandy France. Thousands die in the first hours. But the invasion turns the tide of the Second World War and begins the liberation of Western Europe.

headline: Atomic bombs dropped on Japan
context: August 6 and 9 1945. The United States drops atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Over 200000 people die — most of them civilians. World War Two ends. The nuclear age and its shadow of annihilation begins.

headline: Jackie Robinson breaks baseball color barrier
context: April 15 1947. Jackie Robinson takes the field for the Brooklyn Dodgers becoming the first Black player in Major League Baseball. He endures death threats abuse and silence from teammates. He plays on. He never stops.

headline: Brown v Board of Education
context: May 17 1954. The US Supreme Court rules racial segregation in public schools unconstitutional. A landmark decision that challenges a century of Jim Crow laws. The civil rights movement gains its most powerful legal weapon.

headline: Rosa Parks refuses to give up her seat
context: December 1 1955. Rosa Parks refuses to give up her bus seat to a white passenger in Montgomery Alabama. She is arrested. The Montgomery Bus Boycott begins and lasts 381 days. The modern civil rights movement has found its spark.

headline: Martin Luther King I Have a Dream
context: August 28 1963. Two hundred fifty thousand people gather at the Lincoln Memorial. Martin Luther King delivers his I Have a Dream speech. A vision of a nation where children are judged not by the color of their skin but by the content of their character.

headline: JFK assassinated in Dallas
context: November 22 1963. President John F Kennedy is shot and killed in Dallas Texas. A nation loses its symbol of hope and youth. His unfinished dream — putting a man on the moon — would be completed six years later.

headline: Martin Luther King assassinated
context: April 4 1968. Martin Luther King is shot and killed on the balcony of the Lorraine Motel in Memphis Tennessee. Riots break out in 100 American cities. James Brown goes on television to calm the crowds. A prophet is silenced.

headline: Neil Armstrong walks on the moon
context: July 20 1969. Apollo 11 lands on the Sea of Tranquility. Neil Armstrong becomes the first human to walk on the moon. That's one small step for man one giant leap for mankind. Eight years after Kennedy's challenge. Humanity looks up.

headline: Bloody Sunday Northern Ireland
context: January 30 1972. British soldiers shoot 28 unarmed civil rights protesters in Derry Northern Ireland. Fourteen die. The Troubles deepen. A Sunday that would haunt a generation.

headline: Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak found Apple
context: April 1 1976. Two young men in a California garage change the world. The personal computer revolution begins. Within decades a device in every pocket would reshape how all of humanity communicates.

headline: First Space Shuttle flight
context: April 12 1981. Columbia lifts off. The Space Shuttle era begins. Humanity builds a reusable spacecraft — part rocket part glider. The dream of routine access to space takes one more step forward.

headline: Bhopal disaster India
context: December 3 1984. A chemical plant in Bhopal India leaks toxic gas overnight. Over 15000 people die in the world's worst industrial disaster. Hundreds of thousands are permanently injured. The planet bears the cost of human carelessness.

headline: Chernobyl nuclear disaster
context: April 26 1986. Reactor 4 at the Chernobyl nuclear plant explodes. Thirty one die immediately. Hundreds of thousands are evacuated. Radiation spreads across Europe. A 30km exclusion zone remains uninhabitable for centuries.

headline: Berlin Wall falls
context: November 9 1989. East Germans flood the checkpoints. The Berlin Wall — symbol of Cold War division — begins to crumble. Families separated for 28 years are reunited. The Iron Curtain falls. The world exhales.

headline: Nelson Mandela freed after 27 years
context: February 11 1990. Nelson Mandela walks out of Victor Verster Prison after 27 years. He does not speak of bitterness. He speaks of reconciliation. South Africa begins its long walk to freedom. Apartheid's end is in sight.

headline: Gulf War begins
context: August 2 1990. Iraq invades Kuwait. A coalition of 35 nations responds. The Gulf War kills hundreds of thousands. Ancient land scarred. Oil wells burn for months. Children caught in the machinery of power they did not choose.

headline: Rodney King riots Los Angeles
context: April 29 1992. Four police officers acquitted for beating Rodney King despite video evidence. Los Angeles erupts. 63 people die. 2000 injured. $1 billion in damage. Can you hear me? Am I a person to you?

headline: Rwandan genocide
context: April 7 1994. In 100 days 800000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu are killed. The world watches. The world does nothing. The fastest mass killing in recorded history. Humanity at its most monstrous.

headline: Oklahoma City bombing
context: April 19 1995. A truck bomb destroys the Alfred P Murrah Federal Building. 168 people die including 19 children in the daycare center. Domestic terrorism shatters the feeling of safety in the American heartland.

headline: Dolly the sheep cloned
context: February 22 1997. Scientists announce the successful cloning of Dolly the sheep — the first mammal cloned from an adult cell. The boundary between life and technology shifts. The question of what we should do with what we can do hangs in the air.
